According to the equations, a better control of both nucleation and crystal growth can be done and the energy required to achieve target supersaturation conditions (temperature and pressure) can be reduced along with better control of the characteristics of crystallized material. US technology has found diverse applications in the preservation and processing of various foodstuffs and pharmaceuticalsBaillon et al. [5]. In particular, US freezing has proven effective for high-value food products and pharmaceuticals due to its precise control and rapid freezing capabilities. Additionally, US has been successfully utilized in the crystallization of food products, such as triglyceride oils (including vegetable oils), ice cream, and milk fat [26,69,118]. One notable advantage of US in crystallization processes is its ability to prevent the encrustation of crystals on cooling elements, ensuring efficient heat transfer throughout the cooling process. This not only enhances product quality but also contributes to the overall efficiency and reliability of the production process.

Drying is a commonly used to enhance the stability of fresh harvest biomass. However, the quality of biomass can be significantly affected by the drying process, particularly when high temperatures or long drying times are employed [16]. Low-cost methods like solar drying require ample spaces and favourable climate conditions, while other traditional methods, such as hot air drying, though widely used in the industry, are energy-intensive, time-consuming, and involve high temperatures. These factors can potentially compromise the quality of the final products and thus, advanced drying technologies, such as freeze-drying, fluidized bed drying, microwave drying, and spray drying have been developed for specific applications. However, these new methods can often be time- or energy- consuming processes that could be improved, and US emerges as a potentially new drying alternative due to several mechanisms: (i) Cavitation induces acoustic streaming and microstreaming in the drying medium or material being dried. These streaming effects disrupt the boundary layer of moisture near the surface, reducing the stagnant air or vapour film thickness, promoting a fast and efficient mass transfer of moisture from the material to the surrounding drying medium, resulting in accelerated drying rates Zhu et al. [156], Zhu et al. [157]; (ii) US is able to increase the surface area of the target material as the cavitation generates bubbles on the surface of the material or within the drying medium. These bubbles collapse violently, leading to the generation of localized turbulences and small droplets or microbubbles. The increased surface area due to these microbubbles enhances the contact between the drying medium and the material, facilitating more efficient moisture evaporation [151]; (iii) Ultrasonic waves can disrupt the diffusion pathways within the material being dried [13]. The mechanical vibrations induced by US can help to break down the structural barriers, such as cell walls or barriers between moisture-containing regions, allowing moisture to move more freely and accelerating the drying; (iv) Ultrasonic waves can generate localized heating due to the absorption of sound energy by the material. This localized heating can contribute to faster moisture evaporation and drying Zhu et al. [154], Zhu et al. [155].

Additionally, US can help in distributing the heat more evenly within the material, reducing the risk of overheating or localized hotspots during drying. Ultrasonic waves can prevent or minimize case hardening, which is the formation of a dry outer layer that hinders moisture evaporation from the inner layers. Zhu et al. [156], Zhu et al. [157]. The mechanical agitation provided by US disrupts the surface layer and prevents the formation of a moisture barrier, allowing uniform and efficient drying [123]. US-assisted drying can also preserve the quality of the dried products by reducing the drying time and operating at lower temperatures, minimizing heat-induced degradation, colour changes, loss of volatile compounds, total polyphenols, and other quality deterioration that can occur during traditional drying methods. Thereby, Zhu et al. [156], Zhu et al. [157] employed both US probe and air-borne US assisted fluidized bed drying to dry seaweed samples. The researchers reported that the US-assisted drying process induced lower colour changes and preserved total polyphenol contents in the dried samples when compared to conventional hot air drying Zhu et al. [156], Zhu et al. [157]. Pei et al. [95] explored the use of multiple temperatures when drying saffron by far-infrared assisted US. They found that 30 s US with far-infrared drying at 50 °C maintained a better quality of the dried saffron in terms of their profile of volatile compounds compared to that of far-infrared drying alone.

In order to scale-up US-assisted drying processes, the drying kinetics need to be well established. To model the water transfer during US-enhanced drying, various approaches can be considered, including diffusional models based on Fick's law with varying degrees of complexity and several empirical models. While these models do not explicitly incorporate ultrasonic factors, they can account for the influence of US when drying by adjusting the kinetic parameters. For the diffusion models, the temperature is considered to be uniform, and both the sample volume and effective moisture diffusivity are constant during drying. The governing equation for cube geometry is shown in Eq. (12).
(13)

Another modelling strategy is to use empirical models. Empirical models,such as Newton, Henderson and Pabis, Weibull, Page, Midilli et al., etc. are widely employed and studied to simulate US-assisted drying processes for food products [73,126],Zhu et al. [156], Zhu et al. [157]. These models were successfully employed for drying different materials. Compared to the diffusion models, the computation of empirical models is much easier. However, the use of empirical models to simulate US-assisted drying does not provide insights into the underlying mechanisms of mass transfer during the drying process. Additionally, empirical models rely exclusively on product properties or working conditions, limiting their explanatory power. Therefore, it is recommended to consider both empirical and diffusion models when modelling the US-assisted drying processes to provide a more comprehensive understanding of the mass transfer mechanisms underlying these effects.

Emulsification is an important means of delivering hydrophobic bioactive compounds into a range of food products [140]. Acoustic emulsification produces stable particles in the submicron range with an extremely narrow particle size distribution using a smaller emulsifier ratio and lower energy compared to conventional methods. However, when using acoustic emulsification, it is important to choose an appropriate frequency for each application. Low frequencies, such as 20 kHz, can generate strong shear forces due to the violent nature of bubble collapse. In contrast, US with high frequencies generates weaker shear forces that are not suitable for emulsification. Only high-intensity, low-frequency US (between 16 and 100 kHz) are capable of producing emulsions [18].

It is essential to understand the effects of US on emulsions. Physically, cavitation generates high-pressure that can severely damage solid particles suspended in liquids and large emulsion droplets. US propagation also produces physical effects, as it travels through a medium in the form of mechanical energy, causing vibration effects on particles. Since emulsions are not homogeneous media, the passage of ultrasonic waves causes different vibration speeds in particles, leading to pressure changes due to their relative movement, which can alter flow characteristics of the emulsions. Additionally, cavitation generates instantaneous heat that affects the protein unfolding [32], polysaccharide properties [78], and oil oxidation [103], influencing the final characteristics of the emulsions. Chemically, cavitation produces hydroxyl ions (OH•), a strong oxidant that can oxidize any material in emulsions or react with each other to form hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Free radicals and H2O2 can also react with polyunsaturated fatty acids of lipids and oxidize natural emulsifiers and oil phases exposed to them [98]. Several advantages have been described when using US-assisted emulsification compared to the conventional processes [153]: (i) Effective droplet size reduction: US treatments significantly decreases the droplet size and narrows the size distribution of food emulsions. This effect is primarily attributed to the physical effects produced by US; (ii) Enhanced zeta potential: Acoustic emulsification has the ability to increase the absolute zeta potential of certain food emulsions. This may be due to the formation of smaller emulsifier-coated oil droplets, resulting in more exposed charged groups; (iii) Improved flow properties: Acoustic emulsification leads to a decrease in apparent viscosity and consistency coefficient for most food emulsions, while increasing the flow index. The increased flow index and decreased apparent viscosity are mainly attributed to the reduced size of emulsion droplets. US processing has been confirmed to be applicable for many kinds of starches (corn, potato, tapioca, and sweet potato) and polysaccharides for viscosity reduction [54]; (iv) Enhanced emulsifying properties: Acoustic emulsification significantly increases the emulsifying properties of food emulsions, which can be attributed to the decreased droplet size and increased absolute zeta potential; (v) Protein unfolding and enhanced activity: High-intensity acoustic emulsification effectively unfolds protein molecules and increases the biological activity of lipophilic compounds without significantly influencing oil profiles.

Filtration is a common process in the food industry regularly used to separate solid particles from liquid. In traditional filtration processes, the separation of the particulate matter or suspended solids from a fluid relied on passing these mixtures through a filter medium, which can be a porous membrane or a bed of granular material. However, these processes often encounter challenges, such as membrane fouling and concentration polarization, which significantly reduced the filtration efficiency. Membrane fouling refers to the decline in permeate flux caused by the formation of a cake layer or blockage of the membrane pores, problem that can be addressed by US. The agitation and cavitation generated by US play a crucial role in dislodging and breaking up particles that may pose challenges for conventional filtration methods [74]. US can facilitate membrane cleaning by creating shear forces that facilitate the detachment of foulants from the membrane [46], helping to restore and maintain their efficiency. Furthermore, US can also improve the permeate flux by reducing the thickness of the boundary layer that forms on the membrane or filter surface [113]. The agitation and cavitation induced by US disrupt the boundary layer, enabling faster and more efficient filtration. As a result, US-assisted filtration can achieve higher flow rates and improved filtration efficiencies compared to conventional methods.

Significant efforts have been made to develop models for US-assisted filtration, aiming to provide a theoretical framework to understand and optimize the process. The film theory and resistance in series model can be employed to model US-assisted filtration. The flux through the membrane
J

Ultrasound-assisted extraction (UAE) is a versatile technique that has gained significant attention in the field of extraction due to its unique mechanism, broad applications, and potential for scale-up. It is noteworthy that US can also be employed to assist other novel extraction technologies, including microwave-assisted extraction (MAE), sub-critical and supercritical fluid extraction (SFE), and enzyme-assisted extraction [38,84,86]. The application of US in these extraction methods enhances the extraction efficiency, reduces extraction time, and improves the yield of bioactive compounds from various raw materials. With the consideration of these advantages, UAE has been developed for biorefinery process for agri-food biomass Zhu et al. [154], Zhu et al. [155]. The mechanism of UAE involves the generation of cavitation bubbles under the influence of high-frequency sound waves, which subsequently collapse and create localized microjets, shockwaves, and turbulence within the extraction medium. These dynamic phenomena enhance mass transfer, disrupt cell structures, and facilitate the release of target compounds from the source material Zhu et al. [154], Zhu et al. [155]. In terms of applications, UAE has been successfully employed in extracting various bioactive compounds such as proteins, polyphenols, alkaloids, essential oils, and pigments from plant materials, marine sources, and food by-products [116,47,59,72,134]. When it comes to scale-up strategies, the main focus lies in optimizing process parameters to ensure consistent and efficient extraction on a large scale [139]. This involves considering factors such as US intensity, frequency, and treatment time, as well as the selection of appropriate extraction solvents and equipment design.

UAE uses ultrasonic frequencies mainly in the range of 20–100 kHz Zhu et al. [154], Zhu et al. [155]. High frequency systems have been extensively explored in fat separation processes, with the choice of frequency depending on the material type and biomass structure. Frequencies ranging from 1 to 2 MHz have demonstrated effective separation of small-sized particles. Juliano et al. [61] used a 200 L reactor equipped with 400 and 600 kHz transducers for a pilot scale olive oil extraction and reported up to 1.7 % extra oil extractability with regards to the control sample [61]. The increase of US frequency can reduce the US intensity for extraction, and consequently reduce the extraction efficiency.

The US processing time plays a crucial role in the extraction yields during UAE processes and an optimization of treatment times is often needed to increase the efficiency of the processes [114]. Overall, in most processes of extraction and depending on the raw material, during the first stage of extraction (10–20 min), the extraction efficiency of US may not be significantly improved as the US will require some time to disrupt the structure of the raw materials and facilitate the release of the inner components from the biomass. Longer processing times may be needed in these cases to enhance the extraction efficiency and increase the yield by promoting the transfer of inner components into the solvent. Once this stage is reached and completed, prolonging the US treatment may not be beneficial. The diffusion front moves towards the interior of the raw material, resulting in reduced diffusion area and rate. Additionally, the equilibrium in osmotic pressure between the inside and outside of the raw material may be reached as the diffusion rate decreases [135]. Therefore, the extraction yield does not improve further with extended US treatment and longer processing times can also potentially lead to the degradation of the extracted compounds.

As previously mentioned, the amount of ultrasonic energy transferred to the solvent is crucial in most extraction processes. Parameters such as US power (W), US intensity (W/cm2), and acoustic energy density (W/mL) are used to quantify the dissipated ultrasonic energy in the system. Similarly to the effects of temperature and time, the extraction yields often exhibit a gradual increase to a peak level and then either decreases or remains stable as US power/intensity increases [19]. High US power promotes the formation and collapse of cavitation bubbles, leading to vigorous shockwaves and high-speed microstreaming that accelerate the disruption of the surface of the raw materials and the release of compounds. However, excessive US power may decrease the efficiency of energy transfer to the solvent due to the excessive generation of cavitation bubbles. Additionally, high US power can potentially cause chemical decomposition of the extracted compounds [147]. It is essential to strike a balance to achieve optimal extraction efficiency without compromising the integrity of the target compounds in UAE processes.

US has emerged as a promising technology in the agri-food industry, offering multiple advantages, such as improved extraction efficiency, enhanced mass transfer, and increased product quality. However, its successful implementation at an industrial scale requires addressing certain limitations. One significant challenge is the high energy consumption associated with high-intensity US, which can lead to increased production costs. To overcome this, researchers and engineers must focus on optimizing process parameters and designing more energy-efficient equipment. Additionally, ensuring uniformity and consistency in large-scale applications is crucial, considering the variations in raw materials and processing conditions. Advanced monitoring and control systems can aid in achieving reliable results. Moreover, the safety and compatibility of US with different food products need to be thoroughly assessed, as its application may induce undesirable changes in texture, taste or chemicals. Further studies are essential to mitigate these limitations and establish overall safety of US based food processing systems. Despite these challenges, with the development of scale-up strategies, US has the potential to revolutionize the food industry by delivering more efficient and high-quality food processing solutions.

Acoustic cavitation has emerged as a promising and versatile technology with significant potential to revolutionize various aspects of food processing. This comprehensive review has shed light on the underlying mechanisms, design considerations, and challenges associated with up-scaling acoustic cavitation for food applications. While acoustic cavitation has shown success at laboratory scale level for several applications, further validation of these promising results is needed at pilot and industrial scales. Addressing up-scaling challenges, including infrastructure costs, energy efficiency, and US power intensity distribution, will be critical to its successful implementation as discussed in this review and several strategies proposed could be potentially used to solve some of these issues.

Efforts to optimize the design of ultrasonic equipment, tailored to specific food processes, are of paramount importance in maximizing cavitation intensity and efficiency. A thorough understanding of the parameters related to the physical acoustic wave, mechanical design, operational processes, and products is essential in this pursuit. Additionally, selecting an appropriate mathematical model and integrating computational techniques for specific food processes will further enhance efficiency and control.

Innovative designs for US chambers, such as multi-transducers, multi-frequencies, and larger processing surface areas, can ensure uniformity and consistency in large-scale agri-food processing applications. However, addressing the challenge of high energy consumption is crucial in achieving practical and cost-effective implementation of cavitation technologies in the food industry. To fully harness the transformative potential of acoustic cavitation in the agri-food industry, continuous research and interdisciplinary collaborations are imperative.

Xianglu Zhu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Rahel Suchintita Das: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Mysore Lokesh Bhavya: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Marco Garcia-Vaquero: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Brijesh K.
